title: Edit your product taxonomy

publish.description: Define the dimensions qSortBy sorts on — Category, Material, Season, Occasion, Fit — with their metafield keys, allowed values, and coverage bars.

intro: The **Product taxonomy** editor defines the dimensions qSortBy uses to understand and group your catalog. This guide walks you through reading a dimension, adding your own, and saving the taxonomy back to your store.

steps.heading: Read a dimension
steps.body: This editor lives one level in: **Left menu → Settings → Product taxonomy**. It ships with five dimensions — **Category**, **Material**, **Season**, **Occasion** and **Fit**. Each maps to a Shopify **metafield key**, is either **single-** or **multi-select**, and lists its **allowed values**. A **catalog-coverage bar** shows how many of your products already carry that data.

els.text: It's a sub-page: Left menu → Settings → Product taxonomy. Each dimension has a metafield key, a single/multi-select kind, allowed values, and a coverage bar.

notes.text: Higher coverage means richer sorts — fill the matching metafields on your products in Shopify admin to raise it.

steps.heading: Add a dimension — or let AI suggest one
steps.body: Select **Add dimension** to create your own — give it a name, a metafield key, choose single- or multi-select, and list its allowed values. Short on ideas? Choose **Suggest with AI** and qSortBy proposes dimensions and values based on your actual catalog.

els.text: Add dimension builds one by hand; Suggest with AI proposes dimensions and values from your catalog.

steps.heading: Save the taxonomy
steps.body: When your dimensions look right, select **Save taxonomy**. qSortBy provisions the matching metafield definitions in your Shopify admin so you (or your team) can start filling them in per product — and the coverage bars climb as you do.

els.text: Save taxonomy provisions the metafield definitions in Shopify admin so you can fill them per product.

outro: That's the taxonomy editor: read the built-in dimensions, add your own or generate them with AI, then save to provision the metafields. The richer and more complete your taxonomy, the sharper qSortBy's sorting.
